content:
First note that if \(\deg f=n\) then the left‐hand side
\[
f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)
\]
has degree \(n\), while the right‐hand side
\[
\frac{f(x)^2}{2013\,x}
\]
has degree \(2n-1\), unless \(f\equiv0\).  Since \(f\) is nonconstant we cannot have \(n=0\), and for \(n\ge2\) we get
\[
2n-1>n,
\]
impossible for a polynomial identity.  Hence \(n=1\) and
\[
f(x)=ax+b.
\]
Plugging into
\[
f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)
\;=\;\frac{f(x)^2}{2013\,x}
\]
gives
\[
a(x-1)+b\;+\;ax+b\;+\;a(x+1)+b
\;=\;\frac{(ax+b)^2}{2013\,x},
\]
i.e.
\[
3ax+3b \;=\;\frac{a^2x^2+2ab\,x+b^2}{2013\,x}.
\]
Clearing denominators,
\[
2013x\,(3ax+3b)=a^2x^2+2ab\,x+b^2,
\]
so
\[
6039a\,x^2+6039b\,x=a^2x^2+2ab\,x+b^2.
\]
Matching coefficients:

1) \(x^2\)-term: \(6039a=a^2\) ⇒ \(a=0\) or \(a=6039\).  
   \(a=0\) gives a constant \(f\), not allowed, so \(a=6039\).

2) \(x\)-term: \(6039b=2ab=2\cdot6039\,b\) ⇒ \(6039b=12078b\) ⇒ \(b=0\).

3) constant term forces \(b^2=0\), consistent.

Hence the unique nonconstant solution is
\[
f(x)=6039\,x,
\]
so
\[
f(1)=6039.
\]
Therefore the sum of all possible values of \(f(1)\) is \(\boxed{6039}\).

\[

\;=\;\frac{(ax+b)^2}{2013\,x},

\[
